summary: Before I enlisted in the service, I worked as an order picker for Montgomery Ward on Chicago Avenue. It was one of my first jobs, and I was living in Chicago at the time. After the war, I moved on to other work, but Montgomery Ward was where I started my working life.

After I got married in 1950, I worked as an accountant for Mount Sinai Hospital. It was a significant part of my post-war career before I moved into public accounting and then government work. Mount Sinai gave me valuable experience in accounting that helped me later on.

I worked for the Internal Revenue Service for twenty-five years, doing a lot of public relations work. I even had a radio show for five years where I coordinated careers for government agencies and gave talks to schools in Chicago. It was a rewarding career until I retired in 1987.

I enlisted in the United States Army Air Forces in early 1942 because I wanted to be a flyer. I started flying training in July 1942 but was eliminated after about a hundred hours due to losing my touch from lack of flying in foggy California mornings. I then served in air traffic control in Hobbs, New Mexico, and was later sent overseas to China and India.

After being eliminated from flying, I was stationed in Hobbs, New Mexico, where I worked in air traffic control. It was a stressful but manageable job directing flights and managing landings. I stayed there until I was shipped overseas in 1943.

I went to Santa Ana, California, for my basic training in the Army Air Forces around May 1942. It was an eight-week course where I learned the basics of soldiering before moving on to flight training. Most of the cadets there, including myself, were from Chicago.

After basic training in Santa Ana, I went to Santa Maria, California, for flight training starting July 30, 1942. I flew a Stearman open cockpit plane and learned combat maneuvers there until late September. It was an exciting but challenging time learning to fly.

The Stearman was the open cockpit plane I flew during my initial flight training in Santa Maria in 1942. It had two seats and required helmets and goggles. My instructor taught me combat maneuvers like rapid descents and loop-the-loops, which I found fun and thrilling.

I was living in Chicago before I enlisted in the service in early 1942. I worked for Montgomery Ward there and later returned after the war to work in men's stores and eventually as an accountant. Chicago was also where I did much of my public relations work for the IRS and gave talks in schools.

After leaving Chicago in May 1943, I was stationed in Greensboro, North Carolina, from May to August waiting for my overseas assignment. There wasn't much to do, so we played sports like baseball and football and traveled around the area. It was a quiet period before heading overseas.

In August 1943, I went to Newport News, Virginia, where I stayed for about eight days before boarding a troop transport ship. This was the last stop in the U.S. before heading overseas. From there, we departed on August 29th for the long journey abroad.

Our troop transport ship passed through the Panama Canal on September 4th, 1943, after arriving in Panama on September 3rd. I remember bringing boxes of candy from the PX to help supplement the food. It was an important transit point on our way to the Pacific theater.

Our ship's next scheduled stop after crossing the Panama Canal was Melbourne, Australia. Although the transcript cuts off before describing the arrival, Melbourne was part of our route as we moved toward the Pacific theater during World War II.

New Zealand was mentioned as part of the route or area connected to our overseas deployment, likely in the Pacific region. While I didn't elaborate much, it was part of the broader context of the journey and operations in the Pacific during the war.

Atabrine was a medication we used during the war, particularly in the China-India theater to prevent malaria. Though I didn't go into detail, it was part of the medical supplies and precautions we took while stationed overseas.

The Order of the Golden Dragon was an honor or recognition connected to service in the China-India-Burma theater. I mentioned it in relation to my overseas duty, reflecting the significance of that campaign during the war.
Bombay was one of the locations I passed through or was stationed near during my overseas service in World War II. It was part of the route or area in the India-China theater where we operated.
Lake Beale was a place I encountered during my overseas service, likely in the India-China region. It was part of the geography connected to our operations or transit during the war.

Calcutta was a major city in India where I spent time during my overseas deployment in World War II. It was a key logistical and operational hub for the China-India theater, and we passed through or were stationed there as part of our duties.

Kunming was a city in China that was significant during my overseas service in World War II. It was part of the China-India theater where we operated, and it played a role in the air routes and military activities in that region.
